Next block (Week 5–8, 9–12 weeks to race) progresses into Aerobic Development phase with progressive long runs and tempo builds.

days.coaching_overview: This 4-week Base Building block (14 weeks to Half Marathon race) establishes aerobic foundation and introduces quality sessions at target paces. Week 1 (17.5 km) anchors the athlete's 4-hour weekly budget; weeks 2–3 build progressively to a peak of 20.5 km; week 4 recovers at 15.5 km. Polarized structure: 80% easy (0:04/km), 20% quality (0:03/km tempo/intervals). Two runs per week (Monday, Wednesday preferred days) fit neatly into the schedule, with Pilates (Tue), Gym (Fri), and Football (Sat) as fixed cross-training commitments. Training paces align with a strong 2:00 HM fitness level (0:03/km race pace). After Week 4, the next block (Weeks 5–8, Aerobic Development) ramps tempo work and extends long runs towards 12+ km, progressing toward Peak Training (Weeks 9–12). Monitor fatigue weekly; adjust intensity if HRV trends down or resting HR spikes.
days.weekly_summary: Establish aerobic base with 2 sessions per week on preferred days (Monday, Wednesday). Recent Strava data shows ~15.7 km over 3 activities in 14 days; Week 1 targets ~16-18 km to settle into the 4-hour budget. One quality session (tempo) introduces threshold work.
days.coaching_notes: Athlete is returning from a light training phase (recent avg 5.2 km per run). Week 1 anchors the block at a sustainable base. Monday easy run + Wednesday tempo build rhythm. Cross-training (Pilates Tue, Gym Fri, Football Sat) are blocked; rest on Thu/Sun. Target paces are tight (0:04 easy, 0:03 tempo/intervals), reflecting a strong 2:00 HM fitness level.

days.coaching_overview:
**4-Week Base Building Block (Weeks 1–4, 14 weeks to race)**

**Phase:** Base Building → Aerobic Foundation Development

**Total Block Volume:** 16.0 + 17.6 + 18.5 + 14.0 = **66.1 km** over 4 weeks

**Weekly Progression:**
- Week 1: 16.0 km (baseline from Strava; establish consistency)
- Week 2: 17.6 km (+10% growth)
- Week 3: 18.5 km (+5% growth; cumulative peak)
- Week 4: 14.0 km (−24.3% recovery)

**Training Structure:**
- **Runs per week:** 2 (Monday & Wednesday on preferred days only)
- **Cross-training:** Fixed Sat (⚽ Football), Fri (🏋️ Gym), Tue (🧘 Pilates) — high-intensity football provides weekly anaerobic stimulus
- **Quality sessions:** 1 per week (Weeks 1–3); reduced (Week 4)
  - Week 1: Baseline easy runs only
  - Week 2: 6 × 3min fartlek @ 3:00/km (tempo pace)
  - Week 3: 5km tempo run @ 3:00/km continuous
  - Week 4: 4 × 2min fartlek (reduced maintenance)

**Intensity Distribution (80/20 Polarized):**
- Easy runs: 80% of total volume; @ 4:00/km conversational pace
- Hard runs: 20% of total volume; quality sessions @ 3:00/km tempo/interval pace
- Cross-training: Football (high-intensity) + Gym/Pilates (low-to-moderate) integrated

**Time Budget:** ~4.0 hrs/week maintained throughout; Week 4 drops to 3.2 hrs (recovery)

**Key Adaptations:**
1. Athlete is running ~5–6 km per session at easy effort; baseline block respects this fitness level
2. Recent Strava shows low running volume (15.7 km over 2 weeks) + high-intensity football; plan builds running consistency while leveraging cross-training for race-speed development
3. No VO2 max data available; fartlek and tempo work develop aerobic power without peak VO2 intervals
4. Half-marathon (2:00 target) requires strong lactate threshold; tempo runs (3:00/km) match race pace exactly, building race-specific fitness
5. Monday hard sessions spaced 2+ days after Saturday football fatigue; Wednesday easy runs pair well with light Pilates Tuesday

**What Comes Next (Block 2: Weeks 5–8):**
- Enter Aerobic Development phase (10–11 weeks to race)
- Progressive long runs extend from 10km → 12km → 13km → 14km
- Introduction of marathon-pace (MP) and half-marathon-pace segments inside long runs
- Increase to 2 quality sessions/week if recovery allows
- Target volume ~19–22 km/week

**Recovery & Fatigue Monitoring:**
- Week 4 recovery week is critical for CNS and muscular adaptation
- Monitor HRV trends, resting HR, and perceived exertion; adjust intensity if elevated fatigue signals appear
- Maintain sub-4:00/km easy pace discipline (no "junk miles" at Zone 3)
- Football is high-value cross-training; respect its recovery cost (avoid hard runs Sat–Sun)

**Race Preparation Timeline:**
- Weeks 1–4 (this block): Aerobic base, consistency, threshold development
- Weeks 5–8: Aerobic development, long-run progression, half-marathon pace integration
- Weeks 9–12: Peak training, marathon-pace repeats, VO2 max intervals
- Weeks 13–15: Taper (reduce volume 20–30%, maintain intensity)
- Week 16: Race week (minimal running, race on target date ~10–11 weeks from now)

Build into the structured block with 2 runs/week on preferred days (Mon, Wed). Target 4 hrs/week budget with 2 × 8km easy runs. No hard session this week—establish rhythm first.
